nativeRange.habitat: Homalomena species are generally reported growing along the banks and beds of fast-flowing rainforest streams and on damp forest floors across Borneo, Sumatra, and surrounding regions of tropical Southeast Asia. Many wild populations grow emersed or are only periodically submerged.

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood or other porous hardscape for the rhizome to attach to
moderate flow across the rhizome to mimic stream conditions
shaded or moderately lit areas; suits midground or foreground placement
introduce only into stable, established tanks — sensitive to swings during acclimation

goodWith: shrimp (Neocaridina and Caridina) which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Bucephalandra, Java fern and mosses
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

summary: Homalomena is a slow-growing rhizome aroid from tropical Southeast Asia, sold in the aquarium hobby much like Anubias and Bucephalandra. Many species and trade forms are available. The rhizome must be attached to hardscape rather than buried, and most plants arrive emersed-grown and require time to transition to fully submerged growth.
careNotes:
Homalomena is a large tropical genus of roughly 160 aroids in the family Araceae, native to rainforest regions of Southeast Asia — particularly Borneo, Sumatra, and surrounding islands — with a smaller number of species in the neotropics. In the aquarium trade it occupies the same niche as Anubias and Bucephalandra: a creeping rhizome plant with waxy leaves, often ranging from coral-pink to deep green, that should be attached to lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape using cyanoacrylate gel, thread, or fishing line. The rhizome (the thick horizontal stem from which roots and leaves emerge) must remain exposed to the water column — **DO NOT BURY THE RHIZOME**, as burial generally leads to rot.

The trade carries a wide range of species and named forms, including H. humilis, H. insignis, H. rubescens, H. sekadau, H. 'King of Hearts', H. 'Sub-Hasei', H. Silver, H. Samurai, and H. Jambi. Care requirements are broadly similar across these, though a few trade varieties are better suited to terrarium or paludarium setups than to fully submerged aquarium life.

Most Homalomena sold in the hobby are commercially grown emersed in Southeast Asia. The transition to submerged conditions can be slow and may involve the plant shedding its original emersed-form leaves and regrowing with new submersed-form leaves over several weeks — a process sometimes called 'melt,' familiar to Bucephalandra and Anubias keepers. As long as the rhizome remains firm and healthy, recovery is generally reported to follow. Introducing the plant into a stable, established aquarium rather than a newly set-up tank reduces stress during this acclimation period.

Lighting requirements are low to moderate, and CO2 supplementation is not strictly necessary, though it is reported to encourage stronger growth. The plant grows slowly. Water parameters of pH 6.0–7.5, general hardness 3–15 dGH, and a temperature of 22–28 °C suit most trade forms, broadly matching the warm, soft-to-moderately-hard streams of its native habitat. Several species can also be maintained as terrarium, paludarium, or houseplant subjects and may not adapt readily to fully submerged life.
breedingNotes: Propagation in the hobby is by rhizome division. Once the plant has established new leaves and roots in the aquarium, the rhizome can be cut at a natural bend using clean, sharp scissors, leaving each section with several leaves and visible roots. Each piece can then be reattached to a separate piece of hardscape, or — for species that root into substrate — planted into a nutrient-rich substrate. Sexual reproduction occurs via the spathe-and-spadix inflorescence characteristic of the Araceae family and is occasionally observed in emersed or paludarium setups, but is not a practical propagation method for aquarium specimens.

primary.name: Buce Plant — Homalomena humilis
primary.notes: Used as the effective primary source after the controller-supplied Tropica URL was confirmed not to catalogue any Homalomena species (Tropica's plant catalogue jumps from Hygrophila to other genera). Buce Plant's humilis page provides the family (Araceae), Borneo native range, pH 6-7.5, adult size 4-10 in (10-25 cm), low-to-medium lighting, CO2 optional, moderate growth, foreground/midground or hardscape placement, and rhizome-cutting propagation.

additional.name: Buce Plant — Homalomena insignis
additional.notes: Cross-checked genus husbandry on a second species page. Confirmed Araceae, Borneo origin, pH 6-7.5, adult size 4-10 in (10-25 cm), low-to-medium lighting, CO2 optional-but-recommended, moderate growth, mid- or foreground placement (hardscape or substrate), nutrient-rich substrate preference, propagation by rhizome cutting. Agreement with the humilis page on every parameter that both report.

additional.name: Buce Plant — Homalomena Sub-Hasei
additional.notes: Used for native-range diversity (Sumatra in addition to Borneo) and to confirm that some Homalomena trade forms are essentially terrestrial/terrarium subjects (described as 'compact evergreen perennial' for low-light terrariums/vivariums/paludariums).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Homalomena
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback: confirmed family Araceae, order Alismatales, ~160 species in tropical Asia (Borneo, Sumatra, Philippines, Taiwan) and the neotropics (some reclassified as Adelonema). Used only for taxonomy, not husbandry.
